figures.name: Wakáŋ Tȟáŋka
figures.gloss: The Great Mystery — not a god among gods but the sacred totality, the wakȟáŋ (holy power) that is in and through all things; the wičháša wakȟáŋ Black Elk numbered it as Tȟobtób kiŋ, sixteen aspects that are nonetheless one, kin and circle rather than throne.
figures.facet: The shard that refuses the throne: where most traditions place a Person at the summit, Wakáŋ Tȟáŋka is a *mystery* and a *relatedness* — best rendered panentheist, the holy diffused through the whole, so that to translate it 'Great Spirit' is already to mistranslate it into a face it does not have.
figures.provenance: The sixteen-aspect schema comes through James R. Walker's early-20th-c. Oglala interlocutors (esp. George Sword); 'Great Spirit' is a missionary-era flattening Lakota commentators have long contested. Read with care for the colonial recording layer.

figures.name: Iktómi
figures.gloss: The spider, first son of Iŋyaŋ the Rock and the one who named all things; clever, lecherous, perpetually undone by his own scheming, yet the very being who gave the people language and, in his blundering, teaches the law by breaking it.
figures.facet: The teacher whose lessons are his failures: Iktómi instructs not by gift or commandment but by getting caught — the trickster as a pedagogy of consequence, the cautionary tale that walks and talks and never learns.
figures.provenance: Walker's Oglala texts make Iktómi a primal cosmic actor (son of Iŋyaŋ); the popular 'Spider' trickster tales are the living oral surface of that older theology.

figures.name: Ptesáŋwiŋ (White Buffalo Calf Woman)
figures.gloss: The holy woman who came across the plains to the Lakota in time of hunger, bearing the Čhaŋnúŋpa, the sacred pipe, and the teaching of the rites; as she departed she rolled upon the earth and turned to a buffalo calf of white, black, red and yellow, and the herds came.
figures.facet: Revelation as a woman walking in: the foundational sacred order arrives not from a sky-throne or a slain monster but as a visitor received with hospitality — covenant given through gift and relationship, the pipe a portable axis joining earth and sky in every hand that lifts it.
figures.provenance: Central living tradition of the Lakota; the keeping of the Čhaŋnúŋpa bundle continues. Recounted by Black Elk, Lame Deer and others — a covenant-narrative still actively held, not a museum artifact.

figures.name: Wí
figures.gloss: The Sun, chief of the visible wakȟáŋ and patron of the four great virtues — bravery, fortitude, generosity, wisdom; it is to Wí, gazing into his light, that the dancers of the Wiwáŋyaŋg Wačhípi, the Sun Dance, offer their flesh and their thirst for the people.
figures.facet: The sun answered by sacrifice of the self, not of another: where solar cults elsewhere feed the sun with captives or kings, the Lakota repay the light by giving their own flesh in the piercing — the offering turned inward, the body itself the gift.
figures.provenance: The Sun Dance was banned by the U.S. government 1883–1934 and survived underground; its modern practice is a restoration of a suppressed rite, which colours the available record.

figures.name: Čháŋgleška Wakȟáŋ (The Sacred Hoop & Four Directions)
figures.gloss: The hoop of the world and the cross of the four winds within it — Wazíyata, Itókağata, Wiyóȟpeyata, Wiyóȟiyaŋpata, each with its colour, power and lesson; the camp circle, the pipe's quartered bowl and the round of the seasons all repeat the one figure, the nation as an unbroken ring.
figures.facet: The cosmogram that is also a polity: the quartered circle is not only a map of the cosmos but the living shape of the people — Black Elk's grief that 'the nation's hoop is broken' is a single image doing the work of theology, ethics and history at once.
figures.provenance: The four-directions colour-scheme varies by account and band; the famous 'broken hoop' framing reaches us through Black Elk Speaks (Neihardt, 1932), a mediated text whose poetry is partly Neihardt's.

figures.name: Wakíŋyaŋ (The Thunderbeings)
figures.gloss: The Thunderbirds of the west, winged powers whose glance is lightning and whose voice is thunder, beautiful and terrible, eternal enemies of the water-monster Uŋktéȟi; those they touch in dream become heyókȟa, the sacred clowns who must live everything backwards.
figures.facet: The storm-power that conscripts its visionaries into contraries: to dream of the Wakíŋyaŋ is to be bound to the heyókȟa path — to ride backwards, laugh at terror and do all things in reverse — the lightning's lesson delivered as holy absurdity.
figures.provenance: Recorded by Walker, Densmore and Black Elk; the heyókȟa ceremony is closely held and was among the suppressed rites.

figures.name: Uŋktéȟi
figures.gloss: The great horned water-monster of the rivers and deep places, a power of the waters and the underneath whose endless war with the Thunderbeings of the sky orders the world between flood and storm; her bones, the people said, are the great fossils washed from the Plains earth.
figures.facet: The serpent read in the fossil bed: the Lakota saw the mammoth and dinosaur bones eroding from the Badlands and knew them for Uŋktéȟi — a cosmology that takes the deep-time bones of the land as the body of its water-power, myth as a reading of the actual ground.
figures.provenance: Walker and others; the identification of fossil remains with Uŋktéȟi is well attested in Plains ethnography.

figures.name: Haŋwí
figures.gloss: The Moon, companion of Wí; in the Walker cosmogony she once shared his honoured place, lost it through the trickery of the world's making, and so walks the night — the measure of the months and the woman's seasons, set apart from the day she once shared.
figures.facet: The displaced co-regent: Haŋwí's demotion from the sun's side to the lonely night is told as the very event that set day apart from night and time in motion — the moon's exile as the origin of measured time itself.
figures.provenance: Chiefly from Walker's Oglala mythology, whose systematised theogony (Wí, Haŋwí, Tȟaté, the wind-sons) bears the marks of a single learned informant's ordering.

edges.note: Within the one Mystery, Iktómi is a named aspect (son of the Rock, namer of things) — the totality and its sly intelligence, mystery and the mind that schemes inside it.
Wí the Sun is counted among the Tȟobtób kiŋ, the sixteen aspects of Wakáŋ Tȟáŋka — the visible chief of the holy powers that are, all together, the one Great Mystery.
Sun and Moon, once seated together and parted by the world's making — Wí keeping the day and the Sun Dance, Haŋwí keeping the night and the months; the Lakota day-and-night couple.
The sky-power and the water-power locked in eternal war — Wakíŋyaŋ of the west against Uŋktéȟi of the deep; their unending strife is the Lakota engine of cosmic balance, storm against flood.
The Sun is the centre the dancers face and the hoop is the world drawn around that centre; the Sun Dance is performed within the quartered circle, light and ring as one rite.
White Buffalo Calf Woman brought the Čhaŋnúŋpa whose quartered bowl and four ribbons ARE the sacred hoop and four directions in the hand — the culture-hero's gift is the cosmogram itself.

Two American conceptions of a single sacred power-stuff diffused through all things — wakȟáŋ and teōtl alike a holy energy pervading the cosmos rather than a personal high god; the panentheist intuition reached twice in the Americas.
The sacred totality of which all beings are aspects — Wakáŋ Tȟáŋka's sixteen-in-one answers Brahman's one-without-a-second behind the many; both resist the idol of a single ruling Person.
The unnameable holy whole that is in and through everything and cannot be reduced to a god — the Great Mystery and the Dao alike name reality's sacred ground rather than a being within it.

Both stand behind the working powers as the source-totality, but the parallel is loose — Ometeotl a self-existent dual deity, Wakáŋ Tȟáŋka a mystery and relatedness, not a maker; a faint one-through-many echo only.

Tricksters who are also the keepers of language and the crossroads of meaning — Iktómi who named all things, Èṣù who carries every word between human and divine; the trickster as the hinge of communication.
Culture-hero tricksters whose gifts to humankind come wrapped in mischief and overreach — Iktómi the namer, Māui the fire-snarer and sun-snarer; benefaction inseparable from the prank.
The clever schemer forever undone by his own cunning, half-helper half-saboteur of the cosmic order; Iktómi and Loki are the trickster's two-faced intelligence in unrelated worlds.
Both the restless, deceiving intelligence loose within the sacred order, but the weight differs — Tezcatlipoca a dread omnipotent power, Iktómi a comic primal namer; a loose trickster rhyme, flagged.

American culture-bringers who deliver the sacred arts and the rites of right living and then depart in transfigured form — the Feathered Serpent and the White Buffalo Calf Woman, revelation that walks in and walks out.
The benefactor who hands humankind the founding gift of culture — Ptesáŋwiŋ the pipe and the rites, Prometheus the fire; the civilising boon, though hers is freely given where his is stolen.

A holy woman who arrives among a starving people and whose coming restores plenty and institutes a sacred rite — Ptesáŋwiŋ bringing the buffalo and the pipe, Demeter the grain and the Mysteries; a loose great-mother/culture-rite rhyme.

Sun as chief of the holy powers and centre of the people's highest rite — Wí of the Sun Dance and Amaterasu of the imperial cult; the solar disk as the focus of a nation's worship in two unconnected worlds.

Two New-World solar cults whose great rite feeds the sun with offered flesh — but inverted: Huitzilopochtli is fed the captive's heart, Wí is repaid the dancer's OWN flesh; sacrifice of the other against sacrifice of the self.
The sun as the wheeling holy eye and patron of high virtue, gazed upon in disciplined devotion; Wí and Sūrya are independent solar foci of a sky-revering people.

Thunder-powers of the sky whose voice is the storm and whose ancient enemy is the great serpent/monster of the waters — the Thunderbirds against Uŋktéȟi as Thor against the World-Serpent; the storm-versus-serpent template reached far outside Indo-European.

Storm-wielders whose thunderbolt war with a water-withholding serpent-monster keeps the world in order — Wakíŋyaŋ against Uŋktéȟi, Indra against Vṛtra; the same sky-against-deep drama, no shared line.
The thunder imagined as a living, glancing power of the sky whose touch marks and consecrates certain people; the Thunderbeings and Ṣàngó are independent personifications of the storm's holy charge.

Both name a great serpent of the deep set against the powers above, but loosely — Níðhöggr gnaws the world-tree's root where Uŋktéȟi wars with the sky; included as a faint cosmic-serpent-of-the-underneath echo only.
edges.provenance: The seam is to the Norse underworld-serpent complex around Yggdrasil's roots, not the tree itself; flagged as a reach.

edges.note: Lords of the waters and the deep whose bones and storms govern flood and rain; the Lakota water-monster and the Mesoamerican rain-lord both rule the perilous gift of water from below and above.
Moon-women set beside the sun and made the reckoners of months and women's seasons — Haŋwí parted from Wí, Mama Killa wedded to Inti; the lunar consort/timekeeper arrived at in two American cosmoses.

A loose sun-and-moon-separation echo: Haŋwí's parting from Wí sets day apart from night, faintly rhyming with the sun-hidden-and-restored dramas of solar myth; flagged, no shared structure beyond the day/night divide.
The quartered cosmos centred on a holy axis — the sacred hoop crossed by the four winds against Eden's tree fed by four rivers to the four quarters; the cross-in-circle world-map, twice imagined.

The four-directions cosmogram made the ground-plan of a sacred figure — the Lakota hoop-and-cross and the Mesoamerican quartered board both lay the quartered cosmos flat as a usable diagram of the world.

The world held by its four quarters, each a coloured power with its own quarter of the sky — the four winds of the Lakota hoop answering the four Tezcatlipocas of the four directions, the quartered cosmos in two Americas.
The axis that joins the worlds and gathers the directions — the upright pipe and centre-pole of the hoop answering the world-tree; vertical axis and quartered circle, the same cosmic centring.
